Per cochlea:
• Inner hair cells: 3,500 
signals go the brain
• Outer Hair Cells:  12,500 
sparsely innervated

cell

Hair cells are mechano-electrical transducers
500 nm
Tension on tip link opens 
ion channels!
Leads to nerve firing!
How do the hair cells adapt ?
What changes the tension?

Standard approach:
a)
Figure out the proteins involved
b)
Look at their sequences
c)
Use sequences to develop hypotheses
d)
Test and adapt hypotheses experimentally
From genetics:  
Four proteins particularly important
• Myosin motor
• Harmonin (Ank) and Ush adaptors
• Cadherin cell:cell connector

Actin 
• Cytoskeletal protein
• Forms filaments
➥Extremely well-conserved
➥Ubiquitous in eukaryotes
Modified from Alberts, Molecular Biology of the Cell
muscle
Functions:
• cell motility • cytokinesis 
• cell shape
• muscle 
• signal transduction

The Actin family
• Arp1: “Dynactin Complex”:  
- dynein function
Eckley et al., JCB 147: 307
• Arps2/3: Part of “Arp2/3 Complex”
- nucleate, branch actin polymerization

Human genome:  also extra ARPs…
Not whole story:

➤Structure/function analysis
• unusually rapid divergence
➥loss, alteration of function
➤Help predict function of uncharacterized proteins on basis of characterized
“orthologs”
proteins related by species divergence
→similar functions
Functional information:
➤When in evolution a protein appeared
• ancient, conserved →fundamental part of cell biology
• recent →more specialized

“paralogs”
proteins related by 
gene duplication
→diverged functions
Phylogenetic Analysis
➥Reconstruct evolutionary relationships on basis of sequence similarities
• between organisms
• between proteins

Interpreting Phylogenetic Trees
• Branches:individual sequences
Branchlength: sequence nonidentity
• Tree is “unrooted”
position of ancestral sequence 
is not assumed
• Nodes - inferred points of sequence divergence
1) organism divergence
2) gene duplication
• Positive relationship between time and branchlength
but:
- not defined unless carefully calibrated 
- varies over different parts of tree
Myosin V
Myosin II
Myosin I
Confidence estimates: “Bootstrapping”
• Problem:  which parts of the tree can be trusted???

What is bootstrapping?
Means of determining
Which part of tree can be trusted
Myosin V
Myosin II
Myosin I
Confidence Estimates:
High (> 95% of bootstraps)
Moderate (>80%)
Low (< 50%)
➥“Bootstrapping”
➤Problem: In any tree
- Some branching patterns  
are firmly based on data
- Others are a result of “noise”
➤Solution:
- Take random samples of data
- Build trees from these samples
- See which groups are “robust”

Approach
• Identify, align all actin-related sequences
in all fully sequenced organisms
- PSI-BLAST
- ClustalX  (iterative)
- Better approaches: Clustal Omega, MUSCLE, PASTA….
➤tree is only as good as the alignment
• Determine phylogenetic relationships
• use PROTEIN SEQUENCE!!!
• use only conserved core of alignment
• best to use more than one approach
- neighbor joining (ClustalX)
- also: PHYLIP NJ (PAM250-based)
Bayesian (MrBayes)
• Estimate confidence in observed groupings:  Bootstrapping 
1000 trials

Building phylogenetic trees
➤Three basic methods
1. Distance
a)  Measure pairwise distance between sequences
b)  Use matrix of distances to build tree
- Group most closely related sequences, followed by successively more distant…
“Neighbor Joining” (ClustalX)
- Find tree that minimizes difference between observed distances in matrix
and distances measured along branches
various names “Fitch-Margoliash”
Note:  collapses all differences between pair of sequences into one number
2.  Parsimony
- Find tree that minimizes # evolutionary changes
PAUP
PHYLIP “PROTPARS”
3. Likelihood
- Find most likely tree (based on explicit model of evolution)
Bayesian (MrBayes)
Maximum likelihood (ML)
Note:  2 and 3 are SLOW!!!  Inexact with with large # sequences
3. Combined methods
- Use neighbor joining for first tree, then use ML (“FastTree”, PASTA)

subfamilies
conserved

• 4 additional(!)
subfamilies
humans →plants

Evidence for ARPs in nucleus:
• Yeast ARPs 4,5,6,8 →localize to nucleus
J. Biochem 2000 (Harata) 
• ARP 4 →Members are well-characterized components of chromatin remodeling complex
- biochemistry (humans)
hSWI/SNF (Crabtree)
- genetics (yeast)
NuA4 (Cote) 
• Yeast ARPs 5,8 →members of different chromatin remodeling complex
- mass-spec, genetics, biochemsitry  
INO80 (Wu) 
• Drosophila, human, chicken Arp6
→localize to heterochromatin   
(Harata); (Mooseker)
• Actin itself is in nucleus
- Long but “disrespected” history (!)
J Struct Biol.2002 (Aebi)
- Well-characterized component of
chromatin-remodeling complexes
hSWI/SNF Cell 1998  (Crabtree)
INO80 Mol Cell 2000 (Cote)

Can’t recognize
groups!

Involved in ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling
- nucleosome movement
- nucleosome displacement
- histone acetylation (NuA4)
Also:   bind/modulate transcription factors
-cMYC, others  MCB (Cole)
➥Actin and ARPs ARE in the nucleus,
have been for a long time!
What are actin/ARPs DOING?
• not likely generating force by themselves
• attach chromatin remodeling complexes to nuclear matrix
• regulation?? responding to PIP2 or Ca???
• bind to histones
actin as histone chaperones?  -> “impersonate”histones??
• participate in transcription???
hSWI.SNF chromatin
remodeling complex
Olave Ann Rev. Biochem

Where to go from here???
➤Structure function analysis
• Identify regions of ARPs involved 
in functionally important interactions
• Predict specific ligands
• Actin and ARPs have some common ligands
- bind at a common face
- amino acids on this face will be conserved 
between actin and the ARPs
• ARPs will have some subfamily-specific interactions
- protein faces involved in these interactions will be 
well-conserved within subfamilies,
divergent between subfamilies
Expect:
➥Model ARPs onto actin crystal structure
➥Map conservation onto these models

Structural Similarity:
actin and bacterial relatives

Conclusions:
• Actin has 8 (9) subfamilies
conserved humans -> yeast
- 3 (4) cytoskeletal
Arp1, Arp2, Arp3
also weak group Arp10
- 4 nuclear
Arp4, Arp5, Arp6, Arp8
- 1 both cytoskeletal and nuclear
conventional actin
others???
• Role of actin, ARPs in nucleus is fundamental and ancient
Existence of ARPs highlights importance of actin’s role
• Some protein-protein interactions are likely conserved
between actin and ARPs
Efforts are underway to predict which ones…myosin??? cofilin???

More generalconclusions…
➤Evolutionary perspective can help extract 
structure and function information from 
genomic data
➥Utilize nature’s mutagenesis!
Phylogenetic analysis:  identify
- orthologs/paralogs
similar or diverged function
- when in evolution protein appeared
basic or specialized function
- proteins unusually conserved or divergent 
loss/alteration of function
Conservation mapping:  
Identify functionally important regions
- enzymatic core
- surfaces involved in conserved interactions
